```json
{
  "title": "On-Call That Doesn't Break People",
  "bullets": [
    "Good on-call rotations minimize wake-ups and make responses obvious when incidents occur, reducing burnout and improving team resilience.",
    "Alerting should be action-oriented: if no one can take immediate action, the alert probably shouldn't page and wake people up.",
    "Secrets handling improves with short-lived, narrowly-scoped tokens rotated through automation rather than manual processes.",
    "Healthy systems expect failures and contain them using timeouts, retries, and clear ownership rather than treating failures as anomalies.",
    "Error budgets only work when they change behavior during planning; charts no one references during decision-making are ineffective.",
    "RFCs should structure decisions with context, constraints, options, and explicit risks to turn disagreement into collaboration.",
    "Architecture decisions involve tradeoffs between cost and reliability, speed and correctness, autonomy and consistency—acknowledge these explicitly.",
    "Security is strongest when the secure path is easiest, not enforced through shame or heroics that burn people out."
  ]
}
```

```json
{"title": "Wreck Diving Fundamentals: Calm, Navigation, and Preparation", "bullets": ["Calmness is foundational to safety—it keeps breathing slow and decisions deliberate, reducing stress and improving outcomes underwater.", "Use one primary navigation reference plus one backup (heading plus depth, or reef contour plus current direction) to stay oriented in wrecks.", "Backup lights provide more than redundancy; they shift mental state by confirming you have options, reducing panic in low-visibility situations.", "Master neutral buoyancy through hovering and slow fin pivots to reduce task load and prevent silt clouds that can turn visibility to zero.", "Most underwater stress stems from surprise and lack of rehearsal, not problem difficulty—practice emergency procedures like regulator free-flows until they're automatic.", "The most valuable skill is calling a dive early without ego; knowing when to exit protects both safety and the wreck environment.", "Simple, shared dive plans with clear turn pressures and exit routes work best; complexity increases risk and miscommunication.", "Start with small, repeatable drills and document observations to iterate calmly and build competence over time."]}"
```
